finding: Dual registration: .opencode/tool/ plugin files are loaded as custom plugins via Glob.scanSync at runtime, AND the same tools are registered as built-in Effect tools. Both appear in Tool.Def[] via `[...s.builtin, ...s.custom]` — the LLM sees duplicate tool IDs (e.g. `send_message` appears twice). This creates consumer confusion and splits execution between two implementations. Mitigation: filter custom entries whose IDs collide with builtin IDs, or add a `excludePluginDirs` filter in config.
repair_path: In registry.ts all() or tools(), deduplicate: `const ids = new Set(s.builtin.map(t => t.id)); const filtered = s.custom.filter(t => !ids.has(t.id)); return [...s.builtin, ...filtered]`. Or add an instance-config flag `experimentalSkipPluginDir` to skip the {tool,tools} glob entirely when built-in coverage is complete.

finding: DuckDBWritePool service (Option B) not created — plan recommended a shared DuckDBWritePool Service wired into the Layer chain, but only a raw `duckdb-pipe.ts` with standalone `execDuckDBWrite()` exists. DuckDB-aware tools import this function directly rather than yielding a service. This bypasses Effect service injection: no lifecycle management, no dependency tracking, no test isolation. Tools cannot be tested with a mock DuckDB write client.
repair_path: Create a DuckDBWritePool.Service (Effect service) wrapping execDuckDBWrite, add it to the Layer chain in registry.ts defaultLayer. Have DuckDB-aware tools yield the service. Use DuckDBWritePool=Effect.option for the optional-write pattern.

finding: Tool.Def type signature changed: added `R = any` generic parameter, changed execute return from `Effect.Effect<ExecuteResult<M>>` to `Effect.Effect<ExecuteResult<M>, never, R>`. Non-breaking because `R = any` is the default. Consumers: registry.ts uses `Tool.Def[]` in State/Interface; json-schema.ts imports Tool.Def. Both are internal to the tool package. All 60+ tool files that use Tool.define/create DefWithoutID pass through the default. No external package breakage.
repair_path: No action needed — the change is backward-compatible. Run typecheck to confirm.

finding: Tool.ExecuteResult type is stable — unchanged during conversion. Confirm: return shape `{ title: string, metadata: M, output: string, attachments?: [...] }` remains identical in old and new diff. No breaking change to tool output contracts.
repair_path: No action needed. Document in publication record that ExecuteResult is stable.
